content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
' FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Phaedra Norton 
City Attorney 
City of Turlock 
156 S. Broadway, Ste. 240 
Turlock, CA 95380 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-031 
Dear Ms. Norton: 
April 26, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Turlock City Councilmember Gil 
Esquer regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
May Council Member Esquer participate in decisions involving the reconstruction of West 
Main Street given his home is within 500 feet of the construction project? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Based on the facts you have provided, Councilmember Esquer does not have a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving reconstruction of West Main 
Street due to his ownership of a personal residence within 500 feet of the construction project, 
because the reconstruction of the street will not have a material financial effect on his residence. 
FACTS 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Turlock. You write on behalf of Councilmember 
Esquer who owns real property on W. Main Street in Turlock, California. The City Council will be 
considering approval of a construction project within 500 feet of Councilmember Esquer' s personal 
residence. You have not identified any other financial interest, including any effect on the 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000through91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-031 
Page No. 2 
Councilmember's personal finances, or any financial interest in the prospective bidders for the 
project.2 
The City is proposing to reconstruct and rehabilitate West Main Street from Lander Avenue 
to Walnut Road. Councilmember Esquer and his wife own the property on West Main Street. This 
section of road is currently a two-lane collector with parking on each side of the roadway along his 
property. City staff held three public meetings to discuss several options and to gain a public 
consensus. At the third meeting the following consensus was reached: 
• The road width and travel lanes along and near the Councilmember's property will remain 
the same but will shift approximately 3 feet south. The road will be shifted South to preserve 
the large number of Sycamore trees along the North edge. There is a significant number of 
trees on the North side and very few along the South side. There will be three trees along the 
South side that will be removed. By shifting the curb line south, the City will be able to 
convey storm water on a continuous curb and gutter without interference of the tree trunks 
along both the North and South side of the street. 
• Parking on both sides of the street will remain. 
• The intersection of Grant and West Main will be reviewed to see if it warrants the potential 
placement of a stop sign leading to a three-way stop. 
The Councilmember's property is approximately 220 feet west of the centerline of Grant 
Street. 
The design criteria have not been approved by the City Council. Staff will be bringing the 
public consensus to the Council for their concurrence in the next 45 days. According to Michael 
Pitcock, the City's Development Services Director, the project will maintain the street width, 
parking lanes, and travel lanes, and will reconstruct the street, curbs, gutters, and sidewalks. 
City staff does not anticipate any change in the character of the neighborhood, or any 
substantial alteration of traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking surrounding the official's 
real property. In addition, you do not expect that there will be any changes to the view, privacy, 
noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value 
of the Councilmember's residence. 
The project is currently in design, and you believe that the project is exempt from CEQA in 
accordance with "Section 15301 (Existing Facilities) of the CEQA Guidelines." 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable 
? Should you identify additional financial interests, we encourage you to seek further assistance. 


File No. A-17-031 
Page No. 3 
that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. 
(Section 87103.) 
Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict ofinterest may arise and includes 
among those interests: 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
Based on the information you provided, Councilmember Esquer has a real property interest 
in his home. (Section 87103(b ). ) 
Foreseeability: 
To give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, the effects of decision on a public 
official's financial interests must be both foreseeable and material. (Section 87103.) Generally, a 
financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the 
decision. 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides: 
"A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable 
if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the 
subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract 
with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a 
real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l )-(6)." 
None of the standards set forth above apply to your facts. If the real property interest is "not 
explicitly involved," as here, a financial effect is only reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 
1870l(b).) Based upon the facts provided, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable because it is a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Materiality: 
Regulation l 8702.2(a) uses 12 different standards (including the standards in Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)(l) - (6) set forth above) to determine if an effect on an interest in real property is 
material. 
The factors applicable to your facts are: 
(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage 
or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest will receive new 
or improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are 
provided to or received by other similarly situated properties in the official's 


File No. A-17-031 
Page No. 4 
jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise receive a disproportionate benefit or 
detriment by the decision; 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by substantially altering 
traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the 
official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including 
odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel 
in which the official has a financial interest; 
(I I) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet 
of the property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property 
containing a business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed under 
Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide 
written advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the 
Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under 
the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that 
its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's 
property. 
Your facts indicate that the decision regarding the reconstruction of West Main Street would 
merely shift street features about three feet south of their current locations to preserve a large 
number of Sycamore trees along the North edge of the road. The street width, parking lanes, and 
travel lanes would remain the same. The project would also shift and reconstruct the street, curbs, 
gutters, and sidewalks near the Councilmember's residence. 
The only potential change proposed is to place a stop sign at Grant and West Main Street, 
leading to a three-way stop. The Councilmember's property is approximately 220 feet west of the 
centerline of Grant Street. 
The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, West Main Street. However, 
Councilmember Esquer's residence will not receive new or improved services that are 
distinguishable from improvements or services that are provided to or received by others in the 
area, and his property will not receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision. 
Therefore, Regulation 18702.2(a)(6) would not apply. 
The facts you have provided suggest that the Councilmember's residence will not experience 
issues with view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality due to these project activities. Additionally, 
your facts indicate there will not be substantial alterations of traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, 
or any other factors that would affect the market value of the Councilmember's residence resultant 
from the project's construction. 
Furthermore, the project's mere maintenance of existing street features would not affect the 
market value of the Councilmember's property on Main Street. 


File No. A-17-031 
Page No. 5 
Accordingly, the decisions will not have a material financial effect on Councilmember 
Esquer's real property interest. Therefore, under the facts you have provided, he does not have a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act with respect to the decisions in question. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ECR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
lyn C. Rodriguez 
Se ior Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Council Member Esquer participate in decisions involving the reconstruction of West 
Main Street given his home is within 500 feet of the construction project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Based on the facts you have provided, Councilmember Esquer does not have a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act in decisions involving reconstruction of West Main 
Street due to his ownership of a personal residence within 500 feet of the construction project, 
because the reconstruction of the street will not have a material financial effect on his residence.
